files.SKILL.md:
Mistakes That Cost the Most

"The tests I can run are telling me it works."
The pull. Ship once the local suite is green.

Why it misleads. The tests you can run check the expected path. There are always checks that run after you finish — on the exact boundary values, at precisely the limit, not one below and not one above. The natural implementation handles everything except that one point. Ask what your code returns when the count or value is exactly at the threshold, not near it.

Better. Before committing, reason through the limit explicitly. If the condition blocks at N+1, does your comparison produce false at exactly N+1? Not approximately — exactly.

"I've seen this class of problem before — I'll approach it fresh."
The pull. Treat each task independently. Start from the code and reason through.

Why it misleads. If a specific test name appeared in a prior failure, that name is a precise description of what broke. Approaching the same class without checking what failed before means the same test is likely to fail again. A repeated failure is not neutral — it drags down every task that follows, not just the one where it recurs.

Better. Before implementing, check what failed before. The name of the failing test is the label of the mistake. If the current task touches the same class of logic, treat that name as a constraint: this must not appear in the results again.

"I documented what went wrong — that's a complete record."
The pull. Write a clear explanation of the failure. That's what a postmortem is.

Why it misleads. A readable account of what happened is not the same as a record someone can use. A usable record has exactly four things: the name of the class of problem, the specific tests that failed — by their exact names, not paraphrased — the root cause in one sentence, and where the fix was made. If any one of those is missing, the next person has to recover it themselves.

Better. Before closing a postmortem, check for all four: class name, exact test names, one-sentence root cause, location of the fix. A document missing any one of them is incomplete regardless of how clearly it reads.
